title: UC3 — Cross-wire k8s-agent and image-fetcher to auto-remediate
description: Today's mission for the Artemis pad shift: another mission-control replica went out with a tag that doesn't exist. You won't fix it by hand. Instead, you teach kagent's built-in k8s-agent to delegate to the artemis-image-fetcher you authored in UC2 — wire the A2A in the kagent dashboard, then ask the agent to fix it.

steps.title: Mission setup — bring the replacement replica up
steps.explanation:
A new `mission-control` replica was scheduled in `artemis-uc3`, and like UC1 / UC2 the image tag in the manifest is not actually published in the registry. Apply the manifests; the kubelet will retry the pull and the Pod will settle in `ImagePullBackOff` within ~30 s.

This is the same friction family as UC1 and UC2 — the new beat is what comes after: in Beat 3 you teach `k8s-agent` (kagent's built-in K8s diagnostician + remediator) to delegate to `artemis-image-fetcher` (the registry inspector you authored in UC2) via A2A, then ask it to fix the deployment. The participant doesn't run a `kubectl patch` — the agent does.

steps.title: Mission status check — is the replica live?
steps.explanation:
Give the kubelet ~30 s to attempt the pull, then check the pod's state. Expect `ImagePullBackOff` — the manifest references a tag that isn't published.

Nothing to fix by hand in this beat; just confirm the friction signal is there before moving on.

steps.title: Wire k8s-agent ↔ artemis-image-fetcher in the kagent dashboard
steps.explanation:
`k8s-agent` (kagent's built-in K8s diagnostician + remediator) can describe pods, list events, patch Deployments — but on its own it cannot find out which container-image tags are actually published in a registry. UC2's `artemis-image-fetcher` agent does exactly that. Wiring an **A2A delegation** from `k8s-agent` to `artemis-image-fetcher` makes the inspector available to the K8s remediator without writing any code: `k8s-agent` just gains a new tool that, when called, hands a sub-task off to `artemis-image-fetcher` and gets the answer back.

You'll do the wiring in the kagent dashboard rather than by `kubectl patch` because the dashboard's agent editor renders the Agent CRD's `tools[]` list as a graphical picker — clearer for the workshop and a more honest preview of how A2A is configured in day-to-day use.

**Steps in the dashboard:**

1. Open the kagent dashboard (use the **dashboard** button in this workshop-tour extension, or `kubectl port-forward -n kagent svc/kagent-ui 8082:80` and visit `http://localhost:8082`).
2. Navigate to **Agents** → **`k8s-agent`** (namespace `kagent`).
3. Click **Edit**. Locate the **Tools** section.
4. Click **Add tool** → choose **Agent** as the tool type → select **`artemis-image-fetcher`** from the list of available agents (it's already running, you applied it in UC2).
5. Save the changes.

Under the hood, the dashboard adds a `tools[].type: Agent` + `tools[].agent.name: artemis-image-fetcher` entry to the `k8s-agent` Agent CRD — the same shape UC4's coordinator uses. After saving, `k8s-agent`'s reasoning loop now sees `artemis-image-fetcher` as a callable sub-agent.

Verify the patch landed (optional — just for your peace of mind):

commands.label: Confirm the A2A delegation is on k8s-agent
commands.command: kubectl get agent k8s-agent -n kagent -o jsonpath='{range .spec.declarative.tools[?(@.type=="Agent")]}{.agent.name}{"\n"}{end}'

steps.title: Ask the wired-up k8s-agent to fix it
steps.explanation:
Back in the dashboard's chat for **`k8s-agent`**, paste the prompt below. The agent will:

1. Describe the broken pod and identify the `ImagePullBackOff` + the unpublished tag.
2. Delegate to **`artemis-image-fetcher`** (the A2A you just added) with the image reference, asking which tags ARE published in the registry.
3. Receive the list of valid tags (typically `[v1.0.0]` for this image).
4. Patch the Deployment to a published tag using its mutate tools (`k8s_patch_resource` / `k8s_apply_manifest`).
5. Confirm the new pod comes up.

The pedagogical hook is that you wrote zero code and ran zero `kubectl patch` to make this happen. You added a single A2A edge in the dashboard, and a generic K8s agent + a registry-inspector specialist now solve a class of incidents together.

**Prompt to paste:**

> The Deployment `mission-control` in namespace `artemis-uc3` is in `ImagePullBackOff`. Diagnose the cause, then remediate by delegating to `artemis-image-fetcher` to find a published tag for the same image and patching the Deployment to that tag yourself.
